explanation: ISA stands for Industry Standard Architecture, the 16-bit expansion bus IBM introduced with the PC/AT in 1984. Legacy trivia: the other three option names are not real bus standards.

Answer unverified: the source PDF prints no key for this item, and the widely copied textbook key is 'SCSI bus', which is factually wrong (SCSI is a storage and scanner peripheral bus and has never carried video). The defensible answer is PCI: a monitor is driven by a display adapter card, and that card sits on the PCI/AGP expansion bus, so the path is monitor -> graphics card -> PCI -> CPU. Memory bus and Rambus both serve DRAM, not display output.

In a multiple-bus organisation the general-purpose registers are grouped into one multi-ported block, read from and written to over separate buses in the same cycle. That block is called the register file. 'Register block' and 'set registers' are not standard terms.

With several buses, multiple operand transfers happen in parallel in a single step (for example, two source registers can drive the ALU inputs while the result is written back), so an instruction finishes in fewer clock cycles. A single bus forces those transfers to be serialised, one per cycle. The bus count does not change register size, so B is a distractor.

ISA expansion slots carried the disk controller cards (MFM, then IDE), which is how the hard disk reached the processor on PC/AT-class machines. RAM never used ISA (it sits on the dedicated memory bus), and GPU and optical drives belong to the later PCI/AGP and IDE era. Legacy item.

The CPU is defined as ALU (arithmetic and logic) plus control unit (fetch, decode, sequencing) plus registers (fast internal working storage). RAM and cache are memory outside that definition, which rules out B, C and D.

The CPU is the component that actually executes instructions and performs arithmetic and logic, so it is what does the computing. RAM only holds data and instructions, and input devices only supply them, so 'both a and b' is wrong.

The CPU is conventionally called the brain of the computer because it fetches, decodes and executes every instruction. The trap is picking ALU or control unit: those are parts inside the CPU, not the whole.

Secondary memory (hard disk, SSD, optical media) is non-volatile, so its contents survive power-off, which is what 'stored permanently' means here. Registers and main memory (RAM) are volatile and lose their contents when power is removed.
The motherboard is the main circuit board of the system unit; the CPU, RAM and expansion cards all plug into it. Registers, CPU and RAM are components mounted on it, not the board itself.
The CPU socket and the memory slots are both mounted directly on the motherboard. An expansion board is an add-in card that plugs into the motherboard, and a storage device is not a board at all.
A motherboard is physically a printed circuit board (PCB), also called the mainboard or system board. The other three option names are not real terms.

Results leave the CPU in machine form (binary), and the output unit or interface converts them into a human-readable form such as screen text or print. The input interface does the reverse conversion, and CPU and RAM only process and hold the data.

Answer unverified: the source PDF prints no key, and the item has two defensible readings. Hamacher's textbook phrasing is that the control unit generates the timing signals that govern all transfers, which is why 'Timing signals' is often keyed. But timing fundamentally comes from the clock and answers when, not what does the controlling, and the stem's qualifier 'to control other units' points at 'Control signals': the control unit is the nerve centre that asserts control signals to the ALU, memory and I/O. Adjudication settled on Control signals. 'Transfer signals' and 'command signals' are not terms of art.

In the classic single-bus structure the processor, memory and all I/O devices hang off one common bus, so I/O connects there. Star bus is a network topology, not a CPU bus, and Rambus is a DRAM interface. The item is loosely worded, but single bus is the intended answer.

The control unit fetches instructions and decodes them, then issues the control signals that sequence the rest of the CPU. Logic operations are the ALU's job (B) and storage is memory's (C), which is the usual mix-up.
An algorithm is by definition a finite, step-by-step procedure for solving a problem. Graphs and tables are data representations, not procedures.

EEPROM = Electrically Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory, so erased electrically rather than by UV light (that would be EPROM). The trap is 'Electronically', which sounds right but is not the standard expansion.
